fifa-third-place-2026.selectedSentence: Redemption has a Golden Boot edge: Mbappé chases Messi while England try to turn semifinal pain into a podium finish.
fifa-third-place-2026.fullSpiel: France and England arrive bruised but with something tangible left to win. Mbappé starts on eight tournament goals, while Kane and Bellingham sit on six; England are also chasing their first bronze-medal win after defeats in 1990 and 2018.
fifa-third-place-2026.sourceName: FIFA France v England match preview

editorialPreview.angle: Semifinal recovery meets the Golden Boot race and England's bronze-match history.
editorialPreview.sourceName: FIFA France v England match preview

fifa-final-2026.selectedSentence: The tournament's best attack meets its best defence, with Messi chasing history and Spain one win from a second star.
fifa-final-2026.fullSpiel: Holders Argentina have scored more than anyone; unbeaten Spain have conceded only once. Messi leads the Golden Boot race on eight, but Spain's collective control presents the sharpest test yet of Argentina's bid to become the first repeat champions since 1962.
fifa-final-2026.sourceName: FIFA Spain v Argentina final preview

editorialPreview.angle: The tournament's best attack faces its best defence with repeat-title history live.
editorialPreview.sourceName: FIFA Spain v Argentina final preview

evt_21.selectedSentence: Antonelli starts from pole at Spa, but Norris's 10-place penalty drops the fastest McLaren to P13 and puts Verstappen on the front row.
evt_21.fullSpiel: Antonelli converted Friday's pace into pole ahead of Verstappen. Norris qualified third but starts 13th after his power-unit penalty, while Russell and Leclerc inherit the pressure behind the front row. The title leader has track position; McLaren has the recovery story.
evt_21.sourceName: Official Formula 1 Belgian Grand Prix qualifying report

editorialPreview.angle: A pole-winning title leader faces a reshuffled grid after Norris's penalty.
editorialPreview.sourceName: Official Formula 1 Belgian Grand Prix qualifying report

afl-collingwood-carlton-2026-07-18.selectedSentence: Quaynor returns, but the Pies lose Schultz and Howe as Carlton bring Hayward's wing revival into the rivalry.
afl-collingwood-carlton-2026-07-18.fullSpiel: Collingwood arrive off a tense four-point escape, with Josh Daicos central to the finish. Quaynor is back, but Schultz and Howe are missing; Carlton counter with Hayward in better touch and Hewett named despite a back issue.
afl-collingwood-carlton-2026-07-18.sourceName: Official AFL Collingwood v Carlton match preview

editorialPreview.angle: Selection trade-offs and recent individual form sharpen the MCG rivalry.
editorialPreview.sourceName: Official AFL Collingwood v Carlton match preview

rugby-australia-italy-2026-07-18.selectedSentence: A reset opportunity in Perth, with pressure and transition hanging over the Wallabies.
rugby-australia-italy-2026-07-18.fullSpiel: Carlo Tizzano starts in his hometown as Australia reshapes its XV after two losses. With Joe Schmidt's final Test in charge and an injury-hit Italy arriving, the Wallabies need their accuracy to hold for 80 minutes.

editorialPreview.angle: Selection changes and a coaching transition put Australia's accuracy under pressure.

evt_59.selectedSentence: The Haag is the ambush: 11.2 kilometres at 7.3% before only six kilometres remain to Le Markstein.
evt_59.fullSpiel: Stage 14 starts climbing almost immediately over the Grand Ballon, but the converted forest trail on the Haag is the decisive invention. Its irregular 11.2-kilometre ascent comes late enough to force the general-classification contenders into the open before a short run to the finish.
evt_59.sourceName: Official Tour de France Stage 14 route commentary

editorialPreview.angle: A new late climb creates the stage's decisive general-classification ambush.
editorialPreview.sourceName: Official Tour de France Stage 14 route commentary

evt_60.selectedSentence: Two savage ramps—Croisette at 11.2% and Solaison at 9.1%—can turn the yellow-jersey shortlist into a verdict.
evt_60.fullSpiel: The final 11.3 kilometres to the Plateau de Solaison average 9.1%, after almost five relentless kilometres on the Col de la Croisette at 11.2%. This is not a stage for hiding: the strongest general-classification riders should be isolated before the final climb begins.
evt_60.sourceName: Official Tour de France Stage 15 route commentary

editorialPreview.angle: Back-to-back steep climbs can reveal the definitive title contenders.
editorialPreview.sourceName: Official Tour de France Stage 15 route commentary

evt_61.selectedSentence: The 26.1-kilometre time trial is equal parts climb, descent and flat road—a recovery day only in name.
evt_61.fullSpiel: After the rest day, the Tour resumes with a test designed for complete riders rather than pure specialists. One third uphill, one third downhill and one third flat gives anyone who lost time in the mountains a chance to claw it back, while punishing weak descending or pacing.
evt_61.sourceName: Official Tour de France Stage 16 route commentary

editorialPreview.angle: A balanced time trial rewards complete riders and offers an immediate GC recovery chance.
editorialPreview.sourceName: Official Tour de France Stage 16 route commentary

evt_63.selectedSentence: Orcières-Merlette offers the Tour's displaced climbers one last clean shot at a mountain-stage win.
evt_63.fullSpiel: By Stage 18 the general-classification hierarchy should be clearer, but that creates freedom for elite climbers who have fallen out of contention. The summit finish at Orcières-Merlette invites a breakaway battle while the podium contenders watch each other for weakness.
evt_63.sourceName: Official Tour de France Stage 18 route commentary

editorialPreview.angle: A summit finish balances breakaway freedom against a still-live podium fight.
editorialPreview.sourceName: Official Tour de France Stage 18 route commentary

evt_64.selectedSentence: The first Alpe d'Huez finish rewards early ambition: Bayard, Noyer and Ornon arrive before the famous 21 hairpins.
evt_64.fullSpiel: This is the shorter, more explosive half of the Tour's two-day Alpe d'Huez experiment. An attack can begin on the Col Bayard and carry across the Noyer and Ornon, but anyone who overreaches still has the full 21-hairpin ascent waiting at the end.
evt_64.sourceName: Official Tour de France Stage 19 route commentary

editorialPreview.angle: The first of two Alpe d'Huez finishes rewards attacks launched well before the final climb.
editorialPreview.sourceName: Official Tour de France Stage 19 route commentary

evt_65.selectedSentence: A second Alpe d'Huez summit, now after 170.9 kilometres and 5,450 metres of climbing, is the Tour's final mountain reckoning.
evt_65.fullSpiel: Fatigue is the tactical fact on Stage 20: the peloton returns to Alpe d'Huez after the previous day's summit finish, but this time across 170.9 kilometres and 5,450 metres of ascent. Any remaining podium dispute has nowhere left to hide.
evt_65.sourceName: Official Tour de France Stage 20 route commentary

editorialPreview.angle: Back-to-back Alpe d'Huez finishes make accumulated fatigue the decisive tactical fact.
editorialPreview.sourceName: Official Tour de France Stage 20 route commentary

evt_66.selectedSentence: Three Montmartre climbs turn Paris into a Classics finish, but 15 kilometres to the Champs-Élysées keeps the sprinters alive.
evt_66.fullSpiel: The final stage again crosses the Butte Montmartre three times, borrowing the aggression of a one-day Classic rather than settling for a procession. Because the last Sacré-Cœur passage comes 15 kilometres before the line, attackers and sprint teams both have a plausible route to Paris glory.
evt_66.sourceName: Official Tour de France Stage 21 route commentary

editorialPreview.angle: Montmartre creates a Classics-style fight without eliminating the sprinters.
editorialPreview.sourceName: Official Tour de France Stage 21 route commentary
